Threat.value: Being superalert or watchful or on guard

Memory.value: When I remember the frightening event I feel like it is happening right now.
When I think about the frightening event I can sometimes smell things that I smelt when the frightening event happened.
My memories of the frightening event are like a film that plays over and over.

Negative Appraisals.value: I can't deal with even the slightest upset
I have permanently changed for the worse
I will never be able to feel normal emotions again

vignette:
This 50-year-old married woman from Oceania presented with a high level of post-traumatic stress symptoms following a medical trauma that she still experiences as frightening and intrusive. Her PCL-5 score of 61 reflected the severity of her distress. In day-to-day life she described being easily set off by violence, by talks or photos about snakes, and by people acting suspiciously. These cues were followed by a marked sense of threat: she became superalert and watchful, had trouble falling or staying asleep, and found it hard to concentrate, often feeling tense and on guard long after the immediate reminder had passed.

Her memories of the event remained vivid and invasive. When she remembered it, she felt as though it were happening again, with a film-like quality that played over and over in her mind. She also reported an unusual sensory vividness, at times smelling things associated with the frightening event when she thought about it. These re-experiencing episodes were central to her presentation and seemed to intensify both her sense of ongoing danger and the harsh conclusions she drew about herself. She spoke of feeling that she had permanently changed for the worse, that she could not deal with even the slightest upset, and that she would never be able to feel normal emotions again.

Her coping had become increasingly constricted. She avoided seeing friends, wore heavy make-up, and often covered her face with her hands in an effort to keep others from seeing how distressed she felt. At the same time, the distressing memory intrusions and the persistent sense of threat kept her attention fixed on the trauma and reinforced her negative view of herself. She appeared caught in a cycle in which the memories fed her sense of danger, the danger heightened her reliance on concealment and withdrawal, and the aftermath of each episode deepened her conviction that she was damaged and unable to cope. These patterns were taking a clear toll on her relationships, confidence, and sense of emotional stability.
